The Winter War

# The Winter War

### How the Finns beat the Russians

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Oct 16, 2020

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

Finland is known to most as that peaceful country, with beautiful natural scenery, lakes, Lapland, Santa Claus, Paavo Nurmi and of late Nokia and Angry Birds. Not a country you would really associate with battles and wars. But there was something called the Winter War, that lasted for 3 months, where they actually managed to give a bloody nose to the Soviets.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!B7Yo!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F485f6cf1-dc95-44d2-bbd9-f19e253b597b_300x197.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!B7Yo!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F485f6cf1-dc95-44d2-bbd9-f19e253b597b_300x197.jpeg)

In a sense,the Winter War was Russia’s own Vietnam, a much smaller country, that was no match to it in millitary capability, giving them some severe losses. And all this under sub-zero temperatures( −43 °C (−45 °F). Though the Soviet Union did manage to recoup, and Finland had to cede 11% of it’s territory, it ended up a rather phyrric victory. The Red Army’s rather underwhelming performance, was one of the reasons that motivated Hitler to launch Barbarossa after 15 months.

The background to this was Finland’s declaration of Independence on December 6, 1917 following the Russian Revolution. It was earlier part of Russia, existing as an autonomous unit called the Grand Duchy of Finland, when it was annexed during the Swedish-Russian War. However Russia’s attempts at Russification, alienated most Finns, and led to support for self determination. Also following independence, the country was engaged in a full blown civil war lasting for 4 months, between the conservative, centrist Whites backed by Germany and the Leftist Reds backed by Russia. Which ultimately saw the Conservatives gain power.

Though both the countries did sign a couple of treaties, Finland mostly sought to ally with it’s fellow Scandinavian neighbours, and also supported the East Karelian revolt against Russia in 1921. Stalin already disappointed over the failure of the Communists in Finland, now felt even more insecure with Finnish support to Karelia, which he felt was a direct threat to Leningrad( now St.Petersburg). After the Great Purge, where Stalin eliminated most of his rivals, he embarked on a reconquest of territories. Leningrad being very close to the Finnish border, was seen as vulnerable and special emphasis was laid on it’s security, in the face of a possible Nazi invasion.

When the Soviet Govt requested Finland to cede some islands in the Gulf of Finland, the latter flatly refused. It also made it clear, that being a neutral country, it would not allow Soviet troops to use it’s territory for any armed attacks. Following the Molotov-Ribbentrop pact that gave the Soviet Union full control over the East, they began to demand that Finland destroy all it’s fortifications on Karelian Isthmus, ceded islands in Gulf of Finland, as well as Rybachy Peninsula. As also to lease the Hanko Peninsula for 30 years, allowing the Soviets to set up a military base there.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vYFS!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff369a2d3-a0fb-457f-9ce5-3c396d71dae8_220x144.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vYFS!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff369a2d3-a0fb-457f-9ce5-3c396d71dae8_220x144.jpeg)

Finland rejected the demands outright, and when the Red Army shelled the border village of Mainila in November 1939, and accused the Finnish forces of doing it, the war began.

Following their conquest of Eastern Poland, Stalin expected it to be an easy victory in Finland, lasting just a couple of weeks. The fact is Stalin’s Purges, had hit the officers section of the Red Army, very hard. 3 Marshals, 220 high level commanders and around 36,000 officers were purged out, leaving the Army only with yes men soldiers, who were not even barely competent. Commanders were choosen based on their political loyalties rather than merit.

The Finnish army on the other hand was woefully under equipped,it’s soldiers did not even have proper uniforms. There was a shortage of ammunition, and fuel stocks too were low. Add to it the country’s rather low population meant they had to use every hand on deck, unlike the Soviets who had excess of men at their disposal. From every angle, it looked like a total rout for Finland.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!nSZT!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F27ebdb6b-6c88-4c78-b427-96602bf6127e_220x216.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!nSZT!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F27ebdb6b-6c88-4c78-b427-96602bf6127e_220x216.jpeg)

The Soviets bombed Helsinki on November 30, 1939, and was expelled from the League of Nations soon after. Much like in Vietnam, the following factors contributed to the resolute defense by the Finns.

One was the spirit of National Unity, where all the Finns united against the Soviets. Stalin felt that following the civil war,the working classes and trade unions in Finland, would back the Soviets,and even established a puppet Government. However following the Ribbentrop-Molotov pact, many ordinary Finns who till then were trusting Germany to help them out, became wary of both the nations.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PYBv!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fcd4a7666-a947-4860-942f-9e66d40b5afc_170x250.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PYBv!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fcd4a7666-a947-4860-942f-9e66d40b5afc_170x250.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

It helped that Finland was aided by geography too. 1939–40 was a particularly cold year, with temperatures plunging to −43 °C (−45 °F) . The Finns used the terrain to their advantage, as also the fact that were experts in cross-country skiing. They managed to camouflage themselves well in the snow, and execute regular guerilla attacks on the Soviet forces. The Soviets on the other hand were ill equipped for the winter conditions,at least 60,000 of it’s soldiers died of frostbite, nor did they have proper shelter against the harsh cold.

Much like in Vietnam, guerilla warfare played a significant role here. Though the Red Army had the larger numbers, the Finns more than made up for that with deft manoeuvres, and speed. They isolated the largerd Red Army into smaller groups and attacked them from all sides. In the words of William R. Trotter

> The Soviet soldier had no choice. If he refused to fight, he would be
> shot. If he tried to sneak through the forest, he would freeze to
> death. And surrender was no option for him; Soviet propaganda had told
> him how the Finns would torture prisoners to death.

It was not just the terrain, for instance the Karelian Isthmus was not really the ideal place for guerilla tactics. Due to this the Finns had to fall back on the Mannerheim Line, which was not really well fortified, the primitive bunkers would be no match for the Red Army tanks. Again what really counted was what they called their “Sisu”, the fighting spirit. When the tanks broke through the line in the Summa region, the Finns dug out in their trenches, as they did not have sufficient anti tank weapons. They waited for the Red Army to scatter, and once the tanks were well inside Finnish territory, they launched an attack, destroying around 20, and stopping the Soviet advance.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vt9j!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff85b3b6f-48da-4d08-a106-dedb273a18c8_220x210.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vt9j!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff85b3b6f-48da-4d08-a106-dedb273a18c8_220x210.jpeg)

Another was the Battle of Kolla in Ladoga Karelia, where the slogan*Kollaa kestää( Kollaaa holds)*became their rallying motto. Using the ridges, and lack of proper roads, the Finns once again attacked the Red Army, inflicting heavy damages. The battle was noted for the legendary Finnish sniper Simo Hayha, nicknamed as White Death, believed to have killed over 500 Red Army soldiers during the War.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!v1Wz!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd22cfec3-1686-40bc-9941-f5cba285f9ff_219x316.webp)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!v1Wz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd22cfec3-1686-40bc-9941-f5cba285f9ff_219x316.webp)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Kzs0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff0f5c8fc-a870-4d8e-95dc-9baeb8e0c40f_220x139.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Kzs0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff0f5c8fc-a870-4d8e-95dc-9baeb8e0c40f_220x139.jpeg)

Another example was the small town of Suomussalmi-Raate Road, where the Finnish ambushed a much larger Red Army, inflicting severe losses on it. One small unit blocked the Soviet advance, while the 9th division, split the Red Army into small groups called mottis and proceeded to demolish them one by one. The Soviets lost 9000, while the Finns just 400, it was a total rout, a much smaller army humbling a mightier one.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!LyoV!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F53a8b1b8-f408-44a4-ad52-6316235189f9_219x143.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!LyoV!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F53a8b1b8-f408-44a4-ad52-6316235189f9_219x143.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!0fo7!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Faf56fb78-c91a-4dc0-8b88-119d1256f115_220x145.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!0fo7!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Faf56fb78-c91a-4dc0-8b88-119d1256f115_220x145.jpeg)

Even in aerial warfare, though being vastly outnumbered by the Soviets, the small Finnish contigent shot down around 200 aircraft, and lost only 62 of theirs. Finnish anti-aircraft guns, downed around 300 Soviet ones. And this considering most Finnish air bases were nothing more than a frozen lake, a windsock, a telephone sets, and some tents.

The Red Army managed to recoup later, and defeat Finland, but it was a major embarassment for the Soviet Union. A much smaller nation, with far lower military capability, and weaponry, managed to humble them. The Red Army’s disorganization and ineffectiveness was laid bare, Germany gained through it’s alliance with Finland, and Hitlerwas fully convinced of an attack on the Soviet Union.
